Fast forward to contemporary practice and endovascular catheter-based RDN for resistant hypertension is now a reasonable option for select patient groups. The renal nerves are located within the adventitia of the renal artery at a depth of approximately 3–7 mm. The renal nerves, being the target of RDN, are situated in a desirable location for the two US Food and Drug Administration approved RDN catheters, the Symplicity Spyral RDN system by Medtronic, Inc. and the Paradise Ultrasound RDN System by ReCor, Inc. (1).

[methods] Comparison of Ultrasound and Radiofrequency-Based Methods The Three-Arm Randomized Trial of Different Renal Denervation Devices and Techniques in Patients With Resistant Hypertension (Radiosound HTN) trial proves clinical efficacy of both Radiofrequency and Ultrasonic denervation with superiority of the ultrasound-based RDN technique. 12 A meta-analysis of six sham-controlled randomized trials also validated the competence and safety of renal sympathetic denervation as a means of BP reduction (Table 1 ). 23 In 2018, the European Society of Cardiology/European Society of Hypertension (ESH) acknowledged RDN but did not recommend it as a treatment modality. This was primarily because of conflicting results from two major trials. 24 , 25 However, multiple randomized control trials (RCTs) since then show the effectiveness and safety of RDN and are discussed further in this review. Hence, in the 2021 updated guidelines, European Society of Cardiology/ESH proposed RDN as a treatment option with carefully individualized patient selection. 22 Initial failure to show effectiveness was related to technical procedural errors and lack of understanding about renal nerve plexus, but recent scientific evidence shows effectiveness of RDN due to better understanding of the nerve anatomy of the kidney as well as procedural improvements. Whether the cause is nonadherence to medications or insufficient regimen, RDN proves to be therapeutic as an adjunctive treatment in both cases. Furthermore, the Society for Cardiovascular Angiography and Interventions (SCAI) in their 2023 Position Statement and the AHA in a 2024 Scientific Statement addressed patient selection and reviewed optimal procedural and technical considerations for RDN and address it as a promising adjunctive treatment for patients whose BP remains uncontrolled despite treatment with lifestyle and medications. 26 Global support for RDN comes from guidelines of the 2018/2021/2022 ESH, the 2022 Taiwan Society of Cardiology, the 2022 Malaysian Working Group Consensus Statement, 2023 SCAI Position Statement, and 2024 AHA Scientific Statements. All the above include RDN as a potent and relatively safe intervention for resistant hypertension. 22 , 27

retrieved_papers.title: Renal Denervation in Patients With Moderate to Severe Chronic Kidney Disease.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Uncontrolled hypertension is common in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), putting these patients at high risk for adverse cardiovascular events. Radiofrequency renal denervation (RDN) lowers blood pressure (BP) in patients with hypertension. The Global SYMPLICITY Registry DEFINE enrolled over 3500 patients with uncontrolled hypertension worldwide, including patients with moderate-to-severe CKD.
Patients were categorized by their baseline estimated glomerular filtration rate (estimated glomerular filtration rate [eGFR], mL/min per 1.73 m2): eGFR ≥60 (no-CKD, n=2539), eGFR ≥45 to <60 (stage 3a, n=496), eGFR ≥30 to <45 (stage 3b, n=256). BP changes from baseline through 3 years were compared between groups. One-year data for 74 CKD stage 4 patients were also assessed. Safety events and eGFR were reported at all time points.
Average baseline office systolic BP was similar across the 3 comparator groups (range: 162.8±26.4-164.7±24.8 mm Hg). At 3 years, office systolic BP reductions from baseline were -17.2±28.1 mm Hg, -12.1±30.3 mm Hg, and -13.0±27.3 mm Hg in the no-CKD, CKD 3a, and CKD 3b groups, respectively (all P<0.0001). A mild decline in eGFR through 1 year occurred in the no-CKD group but not in any CKD group. Three-year mortality was 4.3%, 7.0%, and 15.2% in no-CKD, CKD 3a, and CKD 3b groups, respectively. Adverse renal events were rare (<1.0%) across all 3 groups. Antihypertensive medication burden remained high in the no-CKD and CKD 3a groups but declined over 3 years in the CKD 3b group (P=0.007).
In patients with uncontrolled hypertension and moderate-to-severe CKD, radiofrequency RDN results in clinically meaningful and durable BP reduction without renal or vascular safety concerns.
URL: https://www.clinicaltrials.gov; Unique identifier: NCT01534299.

[Introduction] BACKGROUND: Uncontrolled hypertension is common in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), putting these patients at high risk for adverse cardiovascular events. Radiofrequency renal denervation (RDN) lowers blood pressure (BP) in patients with hypertension. The Global SYMPLICITY Registry DEFINE enrolled over 3500 patients with uncontrolled hypertension worldwide, including patients with moderate-to-severe CKD.

[introduction] BACKGROUND: Uncontrolled hypertension is common in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD), putting these patients at high risk for adverse cardiovascular events. Radiofrequency renal denervation (RDN) lowers blood pressure (BP) in patients with hypertension. The Global SYMPLICITY Registry DEFINE enrolled over 3500 patients with uncontrolled hypertension worldwide, including patients with moderate-to-severe CKD.

[methods] Methods Data Availability The authors declare that all supporting data are available within the article (and its Supplemental Material). The GSR DEFINE is a prospective, multicenter, single-arm, open-label, observational international registry that enrolled an all-comers population of patients with uncontrolled hypertension. Patients were enrolled from Asia, Australia, Canada, Europe, Latin America, the Middle East, New Zealand, and South Africa. The GSR DEFINE assessed the safety and efficacy of radiofrequency RDN in a real-world setting that included patients with renal insufficiency. Details of the study design and RDN treatment procedure have been previously published. 18 , 28 All study centers obtained approval for the conduct of the study by their ethics committee, and written informed consent was obtained from all enrolled patients. The study was conducted according to the Declaration of Helsinki. Patients underwent RDN with either the first-generation, single-electrode Symplicity Flex or the current-generation, 4-electrode Symplicity Spyral catheter (Medtronic, Santa Rosa, CA). Office and 24-hour ambulatory BP as well as eGFR were assessed at baseline before RDN and at 6- and 12-months postprocedure and annually thereafter. Prescribed antihypertensive medication classes were collected at baseline and each follow-up visit. Major adverse events including all-cause mortality, new renal artery stenosis (>70% confirmed by angiography), renal artery reintervention (performed after completion of RDN procedure and removal of guide catheter), hospitalization for hypertensive crisis/hypertensive emergency (BP ≥180/110 mm Hg, together with progressive or impending target organ damage, requiring hospitalization not related to confirmed nonadherence with medications), and vascular complications (groin hematoma, arteriovenous fistula, pseudoaneurysm, or excessive bleeding) were also recorded at each follow-up visit. Protocol-defined adverse events were adjudicated independently by the Clinical Events Committee (Cardiovascular Research Foundation, NY). Statistics All patients with available data were categorized into stages of CKD by their baseline eGFR: eGFR >15 to <30 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 (CKD stage 4), eGFR ≥30 to <45 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 (CKD stage 3b), eGFR ≥45 to <60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 (CKD stage 3a), and eGFR ≥60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 (no CKD). The eGFR for each patient was calculated using the Modification of Diet in Renal Disease formula (eGFR [mL/min per 1.73 m 2 ]=175×[sCr] −1.154 ×[aged] −0.203 ×[0.742 if female]×[1.212 if African descent]). 29 Baseline characteristics and office and 24-hour ambulatory BP and eGFR changes from baseline to 6 months, and 1, 2, and 3 years were compared between groups. Baseline characteristics, office, and 24-hour ambulatory BP and safety were also assessed through 1 year for patients with CKD stage 4 (eGFR <30 mL/min per 1.73m 2 , excluding dialysis patients). While not included in the statistical analysis, data for the smaller groups of patients with CKD stage 4 are also reported to provide the full set of available data across the CKD spectrum. Continuous variables are reported as mean±SD, and categorial variables are reported as percentages. Comparisons of BP reductions were by ANCOVA adjusted for baseline BP and number of antihypertensive medication classes. To evaluate the risk for all-cause death as a function of BP reduction, a Cox regression analysis was performed, adjusted for sex, age, baseline BP, and baseline eGFR. Groups were defined based on the median BP reduction for both office and 24-hour ambulatory systolic BP. To account for repeated measures of eGFR over time, linear mixed models were run for the no CKD group and a group of all patients with CKD stage 3. The fixed effect covariates included in the models were baseline eGFR, age, sex, diabetes, history of cardiac disease, history of vascular disease, number of prescribed antihypertensive medications at baseline, and smoking history. SAS for Windows 9.4 (SAS Institute, Cary, NC) was used for all statistical analyses.

[results] Results A total of 3294 patients were included in the current analysis: 2539 had an eGFR ≥60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 and were defined as the no-CKD group, 496 had CKD stage 3a (eGFR ≥45 to <60 mL/min/1.73 m 2 ) and 256 had CKD stage 3b (eGFR ≥30 to <45 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 ). Baseline characteristics by eGFR group are compared in Table 1 . CKD stage 3b patients had a higher proportion of patients with diabetes and were on more antihypertensive drug classes compared with patients in the eGFR ≥45 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 groups and had the highest average 24-hour ambulatory systolic BP. Table 1. Baseline Characteristics by CKD Stage Office systolic and diastolic BPs were significantly lower through 3 years post-RDN in all 3 eGFR groups (Figure 1 A and 1 B). At 3 years, the average 24-hour ambulatory systolic BP was reduced significantly and to a similar extent in the stage 3a and no-CKD groups, whereas the ambulatory systolic BP fall in the smaller group of stage 3b patients did not reach statistical significance (Figure 1 C). The 24-hour ambulatory diastolic BP was significantly lower through 3 years in all groups except for the 2-year diastolic BP reduction in the stage 3b group (Figure 1 D). The average number of antihypertensive medications remained unchanged in the no CKD and stage 3a groups; however, there was a significant decline in the number of prescribed antihypertensive medications in the stage 3b group through 3 years ( P =0.007; Figure 2 ). Figure 1. Systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) change from baseline. Office SBP ( A ) and DBP ( B ) measures at 6 months, 1, 2, and 3 years were all significantly lower than baseline blood pressure measures. The 24-h ambulatory systolic ( C ) and diastolic ( D ) blood pressure measures at 6 months, 1, 2, and 3 years were significantly lower than baseline in the no chronic kidney disease (CKD) and stage 3a groups. In the stage 3b group, the systolic 24-hour BP change from baseline was only significant at 1 year, but the diastolic 24-hour BP reduction from baseline was significant at 6 months, 1 year, and 3 years. Figure 2. Number of antihypertensive medications at baseline through 3 years. The mean number of antihypertensive medications prescribed was unchanged or slightly declined over 3 years of follow-up for the no chronic kidney disease (CKD) and stage 3a group, but there was a significant decline in the number of prescribed antihypertensive medications in the CKD stage 3b group ( P =0.007). Safety Renal function in patients with CKD stage 3a and 3b remained stable through 1 year, followed by a nonsignificant decline in eGFR at 2 and 3 years (Figure 3 ). Among patients with an eGFR ≥60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 , there was a significant decline in eGFR at 6 months (−3.2±14.6 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 ; P <0.0001) followed by smaller incremental eGFR changes through 3 years of follow-up (Figure 3 ). After the first year, the decline in eGFR over time in patients with measures at all time points was similar between the groups ( Figure S1 ). Figure 3. Mean estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) through 3 years. Patients with an eGFR ≥60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 experienced a decline in eGFR through 1 year, whereas renal function was stable through 1 year for both stage 3a and 3b patients. After 1 year, the decline in eGFR was similar between the 3 groups. CKD indicates chronic kidney disease. Analysis of the longitudinal eGFR data using linear mixed models confirmed that eGFR declines to some degree over time ( Table S1 ). Among patients with CKD stage 3, baseline eGFR, diabetes, and the number of antihypertensive drugs at baseline were significant factors associated with lower eGFR measures, but only diabetes (eGFR change estimate of −2.47 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 ) was associated with an eGFR absolute value change >1 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 . Adverse events at 3 years are summarized in Table 2 . Three-year rates of death were 4.3%, 7.0%, and 15.2% in the no CKD, stage 3a, and stage 3b groups, respectively. A Cox regression analysis found no significant difference in the risk for death among patients with CKD based on office or ambulatory BP change. Renal artery reintervention or new renal artery stenosis >70% were rare, and rates were similar across all groups (Table 2 ). Vascular complications were also uncommon and similar across the groups. New onset end-stage renal disease and mortality were highest in the stage 3b patients. Table 2. Major Adverse Events Through 3 Years CKD Stage 4 Patients Baseline characteristics for a small group of stage 4 patients are displayed in Table 1 to provide a comparison to other stages of CKD, albeit with low overall numbers and therefore not included in the main analysis. Diabetes and cardiac disease were more common in stage 4 patients than in patients with less severe CKD. At 1 year, the average office systolic and diastolic BP was reduced, whereas the ambulatory systolic and diastolic BP were relatively unchanged (Figure 4 ). The average eGFR of CKD stage 4 patients remained stable through 1 year (22.2±6.3 mL/min per 1.73 m² at baseline and 20.6±10.0 mL/min per 1.73 m² at 1 year). Adverse events at 1 year among the stage 4 patients included 5 deaths (8.3%) and 6 patients (10%) progressed to end-stage renal disease. There were no vascular complications or renal artery reinterventions ( Table S2 ). The average number of antihypertensive medications prescribed was 5.0±1.6 at baseline and 5.1±2.2 at 1 year. Figure 4. Office and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure (BP) change in patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) stage 4. Systolic and diastolic office BP changes at 6 months and 1 year were not significantly different from baseline.

[discussion] Discussion This analysis of patients with moderate-to-severe CKD who underwent RDN demonstrates the continued safety and effectiveness of RDN through 3 years in patients with an eGFR of at least 30 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 (moderate-to-severe CKD stage 3a/b), but possibly even lower (CKD stage 4). Indeed, among the smaller groups of patients with an eGFR <30 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 , there was no evidence of worsening renal function or increased risk of adverse events at 1-year post-RDN. The prescribed number of antihypertensive medications remained stable or decreased through 3 years in all groups. Early work describing the interrelationships between sympathetic overdrive, worsening renal function, and difficult-to-control high BP led to a number of small studies and case reports in patients with later-stage CKD and end-stage renal disease. 23 – 25 , 30 – 33 It was hypothesized that the reduction of sympathetic outflow from the kidneys following RDN would both decrease BP and slow the progression of CKD that is related to increased sympathetic activation. One of the first studies of radiofrequency RDN in 15 patients with resistant hypertension and concomitant moderate-to-severe CKD (eGFR <45 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 ) reported significant office BP reductions with no decline in eGFR or creatinine clearance at 6 or 12 months follow-up. 23 Two other single-center studies of similar moderate-to-severe patients with CKD also observed effective BP reduction and preserved renal function through 1 year 25 and through 3 years 34 after RDN. Furthermore, an assessment of relevant biomarkers of renal damage (neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin and KIM-1 [kidney injury molecule 1]) in 47 patients found no evidence of increased renal damage following RDN. 34 Although several publications from the GSR have reported data on the safety and efficacy of RDN in patients with CKD, most have focused on grouped patients with an eGFR <60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 with no separately reported data on patients with an eGFR <45 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 . 19 , 20 , 35 In the recent analysis comparing 475 patients with CKD stage 3 (mean eGFR, 46.9±10 mL/min per 1.73m 2 ) with 1505 patients without CKD (baseline eGFR >60 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 ), both groups had significant reductions in BP annually through 3 years with a greater reduction in office SBP at 3 years in the CKD stage 3 group after adjusting for baseline SBP (−17.3±28.3 versus −11.7±29.9 mm Hg; P =0.009). Between baseline and 1 year, there was a greater decline in eGFR in patients without CKD compared with patients with stage 3 CKD, but thereafter the changes in eGFR were similar between the groups and not considered clinically concerning. 19 Furthermore, the authors considered the initial decline as likely attributable to regression-to-the-mean. 19 The potential impact of regression-to-the-mean has been well described in an analysis of BP-lowering randomized clinical trials that assessed the clinical relevance of regression-to-the-mean for clinical trials. 36 A recent study of over 4000 patients, including patients from GSR, investigated whether certain patient characteristics, including renal function, had a disproportionate effect on BP changes after RDN using a linear mixed model. 37 Renal function, based on eGFR, appeared to have little to no effect on the efficacy of RDN in reducing patient BP. Although the antihypertensive medications remained unchanged over time in the no CKD and stage 3a patients, a significant decrease in the prescribed number of antihypertensive medications was observed in the stage 3b group, which may have influenced the observed BP changes over time. Furthermore, nonadherence to prescribed antihypertensive medications among patients treated on clinical trials is well known 17 which may contribute more to the variability in BP change seen in the smaller stage 3b and stage 4 groups. This observation of a steeper decline in eGFR in the no CKD group in the first year, followed by much smaller incremental changes, is consistent with our current data. Given that there is an expected progressive decline in eGFR in patients with moderate-to-severe CKD and uncontrolled hypertension, it is unclear how radiofrequency RDN may affect renal function. 38 , 39 A study of patients with 46 hypertensive CKD looked at the progression of CKD from 60 months before the RDN procedure and at 3, 6, 12, and 24 months post-RDN. Linear mixed model analysis of eGFR demonstrated a substantial decline in eGFR in the months preceding RDN, followed by a slight increase in eGFR at 3 months that remained stable thereafter to 24 months. 5 The investigators noted that the BP-lowering effect of RDN appeared to be independent of the observed renal protective effect of RDN. While the mean eGFR at baseline in these patients was 46.2±13.0 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 , our current study also observed minimal changes in eGFR at 2 years post-RDN in patients with a mean eGFR of 38.7±4.1 mL/min per 1.73 m 2 . Further analysis of the change in eGFR over time using linear mixed models confirms expected declines in eGFR in these patients with multiple risk factors for worsening renal function. One notable exception was the impact of diabetes on eGFR reductions, which is consistent with the known association of diabetes and risk for CKD. 40 Among patients followed through 3 years, we observed higher rates of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality in the CKD stage 3b group. The increased risk for death in patients with worsening CKD is in line with the natural history of patients with CKD and hypertension. 41 – 43 An analysis of CKD and risk of death or cardiovascular events reported all-cause mortality of 7.5% for stage 3a and 22.8% for stage 3b CKD patients with a median follow-up of 2.84 years. To put those numbers into perspective, the age-standardized rates of death were 10.8 per 1000 person-years for stage 3a and 47.6 per 1000 person-years for stage 3b. In an analysis of CKD progression and mortality in 5 European countries, mortality rates ranged from 7.5 in Belgium, 4.6 to 22.5 in Italy, and 27.1/1000 person-years in Spain. 42 The United States Renal Data System database reports CKD stage 3 age-adjusted mortality of 48.6/1000 person-years, and CKD stage 4 age-adjusted mortality of 106.6/1000 person-years. 44 There was no evidence of increased risk for renal artery damage after radiofrequency RDN in any of the CKD groups. At 3-year follow-up, rates of renal artery reinterventions, new renal artery stenosis, and vascular complications were low and similar between the patients with no CKD and stage 3 CKD. Although this analysis did not raise any safety concerns with RDN in CKD stage 4 patients, there was substantial variability in office and 24-hour ambulatory SBP changes at 1-year post-RDN in this subgroup. Another concern about RDN for patients with severe or end-stage renal disease is the potential for renal vessel diameters to be smaller than the minimal device specifications. These issues were reported in a feasibility study of RDN in 12 patients with end-stage renal disease and uncontrolled BP. Four of the 12 patients were unable to receive RDN because of atrophic renal arteries (<4 mm), and the investigators noted the challenge in delivering ablations in cases of insufficient blood flow in some patients. 32 These studies were performed with the single electrode catheters, and the latest technological advances with multielectrode catheters may perform differently in these settings. There were, however, substantial drops in office SBP in the 8 patients who were treated. This study also confirmed marked sympathetic activation at baseline in 5 patients who underwent muscle sympathetic nerve activity before RDN. Because minimal data exist, RDN is not guideline-recommended in patients with severe renal dysfunction. Therefore, baseline imaging seems important to confirm the suitability of the renal arteries in cases where this alternative treatment is considered. Limitations Common to observational registries, there is an incomplete 3-year follow-up, and there was often no information about reasons for patients to discontinue participation in the registry. In particular, there was a high proportion of missing 24-hour ambulatory data over time in patients with CKD, which limits our understanding of these data in the small stage 4 and 5 groups. The GSR DEFINE registry also does not include follow-up data on microalbuminuria/proteinuria or albumin-to-creatinine ratio, and the duration of dialysis treatments before enrollment in the registry is also unknown. Perspectives Radiofrequency RDN provided a clinically meaningful reduction in office and 24-hour ambulatory SBP in patients with CKD stage 3a and 3b without any increase in adverse renal events through 3 years. Among the CKD stage 3a and 3b patients, changes in eGFR were not seen for 2 years, and later declines in eGFR were consistent with the natural progression of CKD. At 1 year, there was no evidence of adverse renal effects in CKD stage 4 patients, but there is limited data to support routine consideration of RDN in this population. Our data suggest that RDN is likely to be safe and may represent an effective means of lowering BP in patients with moderate-to-severe CKD in whom other options have failed to adequately control BP.

[conclusion] CONCLUSIONS: In patients with uncontrolled hypertension and moderate-to-severe CKD, radiofrequency RDN results in clinically meaningful and durable BP reduction without renal or vascular safety concerns.

retrieved_papers.title: [Ultra-long-term follow-up of renal denervation in patients with resistant hypertension and mild chronic kidney disease].
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Objective: To investigate the ultra-long-term antihypertensive efficacy, safety, major adverse events, and survival benefits of renal denervation (RDN) in patients with resistant hypertension (rHTN) and mild chronic kidney disease (CKD). Methods: This real-world, single-center retrospective study enrolled patients with rHTN and mild CKD who underwent RDN at Tianjin First Central Hospital between October 2011 and June 2016. Office blood pressure, home self-measured blood pressure, 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure, serum creatinine, estimated glomerular filtration rate, and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio were collected at baseline and at 1, 5, and 13 years post-RDN. The total daily defined dose of antihypertensive medications at 13 years post-RDN was recorded, along with endpoint events during follow-up, including cardiovascular death, all-cause death, hospitalization for heart failure, myocardial infarction, and stroke. Patients were stratified according to CKD stage (G1-G2 vs. G3a) and baseline systolic blood pressure (mild-to-moderate vs. severe hypertension), and follow-up data were compared across subgroups. Results: A total of 40 patients were included, aged (51±15) years, including 26 (65%) males. At the 13-year follow-up, office systolic blood pressure (SBP) and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) decreased by (-32±20) mmHg and (-15±14) mmHg (1 mmHg=0.133 kPa), respectively; reductions in home self-measured blood pressure (SBP: (-25±14) mmHg, DBP: (-10±11) mmHg) and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure (SBP: (-16±9 mmHg, DBP: (-10±6) mmHg) were also observed, alongside a reduction in the total daily defined dose of antihypertensive medications by (1.1±0.9) compared to baseline. Renal function assessments showed no significant differences at 13 years versus baseline in serum creatinine ((105±51) μmol/L vs. (96±22) μmol/L), estimated glomerular filtration rate ((72±22) ml·min-1·1.73 m-2 vs. (78±17) ml·min-1·1.73 m-2), or urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio ((101±86) mg/g vs. (127±82) mg/g) (all P>0.05). All-cause and cardiovascular mortality rates during follow-up were 13% (5/40) and 8% (3/40), respectively. Subgroup analysis results showed that, although CKD G1-G2 patients had smaller reductions in office SBP ((-31±20) mmHg vs. (-34±19) mmHg) and DBP ((-13±10) mmHg vs. (-25±18) mmHg) compared to G3a patients at 13 years, intergroup differences were not significant (all P>0.05). In contrast, severe hypertension subgroup exhibited greater reductions in office SBP ((-55±13) mmHg vs. (-20±10) mmHg) and DBP ((-24±17) mmHg vs. (-13±10) mmHg) versus mild-to-moderate hypertension subgroup (all P<0.05). Conclusion: RDN demonstrates sustained antihypertensive efficacy with favorable renal safety in rHTN patients with mild CKD. Patients with higher baseline systolic blood pressure may exhibit better responsiveness to RDN.
目的： 探讨经皮去肾交感神经术（RDN）对难治性高血压合并轻度慢性肾脏病（CKD）患者的超长期降压疗效、安全性、主要不良事件及生存获益。 方法： 本研究为真实世界单中心回顾性研究，纳入2011年10月—2016年6月在天津市第一中心医院接受RDN治疗的难治性高血压合并轻度CKD患者。收集患者基线及术后1、5和13年的诊室血压、家庭自测血压、24 h动态血压、血肌酐、估算的肾小球滤过率以及尿白蛋白/肌酐比值等数据，同时记录RDN术后13年降压药物总每日限定剂量以及随访期间的心血管死亡、全因死亡、心力衰竭住院、心肌梗死和脑卒中等终点事件。根据CKD分期（G1~G2期和G3a期）和基线收缩压水平（轻中度高血压和重度高血压）对患者进行分组，并对组间随访数据进行比较。 结果： 共纳入40例患者，年龄（51±15）岁，男性26例（65%）。术后第13年随访数据显示，患者诊室收缩压和舒张压分别下降（-32±20）和（-15±14）mmHg（1 mmHg=0.133 kPa），家庭自测收缩压和舒张压分别下降（-25±14）和（-10±11）mmHg，24 h动态收缩压和舒张压分别下降（-16±9）和（-10±6）mmHg，同时降压药物总每日限定剂量较基线低1.1±0.9。肾功能评估结果显示，随访第13年的血肌酐水平［（105±51）μmol/L比（96±22）μmol/L］、估算的肾小球滤过率［（72±22）ml·min-1·1.73 m-2比（78±17）ml·min-1·1.73 m-2］及尿白蛋白/肌酐比值［（101±86）mg/g比（127±82）mg/g］较基线差异无统计学意义（P均>0.05）。随访期间全因死亡率和心血管死亡率分别为13%（5/40）和8%（3/40）。亚组分析结果显示，CKD G1~G2期患者RDN术后第13年诊室收缩压［（-31±20）mmHg比（-34±19）mmHg］与舒张压［（-13±10）mmHg比（-25±18）mmHg］较基线的降幅虽小于G3a期患者，但差异无统计学意义（P均>0.05）；而与轻中度高血压组相比，重度高血压组患者RDN术后第13年诊室收缩压［（-55±13）mmHg比（-20±10）mmHg］与舒张压［（-24±17）mmHg比（-13±10）mmHg］较基线的降幅均更大（P均<0.05）。 结论： RDN对难治性高血压合并轻度CKD患者具有长期降压作用且肾功能安全性良好，基线收缩压高的患者可能对RDN治疗的响应性更佳。.

retrieved_papers.title: 10-year follow-up of patients treated with catheter-based renal denervation for resistant hypertension: an observational study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Catheter-based renal denervation of the sympathetic nerves (RDN) is still not a generally accepted treatment option for patients with resistant hypertension. Data on long-term outcomes, efficacy and safety beyond three years are scarce. We aimed to report the clinical outcomes after RDN in a cohort of 19 patients with resistant hypertension, followed for up to 11 years.
We reviewed patient records from all consecutive patients who underwent RDN between January 2012 and October 2013. All recorded measurements of office blood pressure (BP) listed in the patient file, antihypertensive medications, cardiovascular events, and renal function between 2012 and 2023 were collected. For each year of follow-up (FU), the median office BP was calculated based on all available recordings. The primary endpoint was the change in systolic and diastolic office BP from baseline (prior RDN) to the final year of FU. A secondary endpoint was the change in office BP from baseline to the mean office BP across the entire FU period.
A total of 19 consecutive patients underwent RDN treatment, 10 men and 9 women with a median age of 63 years (range 35-74 years). The median patient FU was nine years (interquartile range (IQR) 8-10 years). The median systolic BP decreased from 181 mmHg (IQR 28.5 mmHg) to 142 mmHg (IQR 23.5 mmHg), with a median reduction of 41 mmHg (IQR 25 mmHg) (p < 0.001). Similarly, the median diastolic BP decreased from 97 mmHg (IQR 13.5 mmHg) to 77 mmHg (IQR 8.5 mmHg), with a median reduction of 21 mmHg (IQR 9.5) (p < 0.001). The changes in BP showed large interindividual variation and the BP reduction appeared to be progressive over time. The antihypertensive medication 10 years after RDN did not differ significantly from the treatment given at baseline. The overall median reduction in total Daily Defined Dose (DDD) was -0.68 and not statistically significant (p = 0.45).
In the present cohort of patients with resistant hypertension who underwent RDN 10 years previously, a sustained and progressive reduction in office BP was observed throughout the follow-up period, despite minimal changes in antihypertensive treatment.

[Introduction] Background Despite the FDA approval of catheter-based renal sympathetic denervation (RDN) for the treatment of patients with resistant hypertension in 2023 and its Class IIb recommendation in the ESC guidelines (2024), RDN is still not a widely accepted treatment option for patients with difficult-to-treat hypertension, often referred to as resistant hypertension in several countries [ 1 , 2 ]. As with any procedure, safety, efficacy and costs remain a concern [ 1 , 2 ]. Current evidence regarding the efficacy and safety of RDN is primarily based on studies with follow-up (FU) periods of up to three years, during which no serious adverse events have been reported. However, data on the long-term effects and safety remain limited [ 3 – 10 ]. The available long-term FU studies are mostly based on selected subgroups of patients who underwent RDN several years ago [ 6 – 10 ]. Although the absolute reduction in blood pressure (BP) after RDN shows considerable variability, an average decrease of approximately 6–10 mmHg in systolic blood pressure (SBP) has been observed [ 2 , 11 ]. No clinical or biochemical markers have yet been found to reliably predict the response to RDN, but the most substantial BP reductions are typically observed in patients with initially very high office BP. In addition, some reports suggest that RDN treatment may lead to a gradual, progressive reduction in office BP during extended FU periods [ 6 , 10 ]. According to recent randomized controlled trials (RCTs), even a modest reduction of 5 mmHg in systolic BP is associated with a 10–15% risk reduction for major cardiovascular events [ 11 – 14 ]. The potential benefits of even modest BP reductions emphasize the importance of achieving effective BP control, especially in patients with uncontrolled hypertension. Office BP measurement remains central to the management of elevated BP and continues to guide both the intensification and de-escalation of antihypertensive therapy in the real world. In this study, we aimed to assess individual long-term changes in office BP in a cohort of consecutive patients who underwent RDN for resistant hypertension ten years ago at a single center in Denmark.

[introduction] Background Despite the FDA approval of catheter-based renal sympathetic denervation (RDN) for the treatment of patients with resistant hypertension in 2023 and its Class IIb recommendation in the ESC guidelines (2024), RDN is still not a widely accepted treatment option for patients with difficult-to-treat hypertension, often referred to as resistant hypertension in several countries [ 1 , 2 ]. As with any procedure, safety, efficacy and costs remain a concern [ 1 , 2 ]. Current evidence regarding the efficacy and safety of RDN is primarily based on studies with follow-up (FU) periods of up to three years, during which no serious adverse events have been reported. However, data on the long-term effects and safety remain limited [ 3 – 10 ]. The available long-term FU studies are mostly based on selected subgroups of patients who underwent RDN several years ago [ 6 – 10 ]. Although the absolute reduction in blood pressure (BP) after RDN shows considerable variability, an average decrease of approximately 6–10 mmHg in systolic blood pressure (SBP) has been observed [ 2 , 11 ]. No clinical or biochemical markers have yet been found to reliably predict the response to RDN, but the most substantial BP reductions are typically observed in patients with initially very high office BP. In addition, some reports suggest that RDN treatment may lead to a gradual, progressive reduction in office BP during extended FU periods [ 6 , 10 ]. According to recent randomized controlled trials (RCTs), even a modest reduction of 5 mmHg in systolic BP is associated with a 10–15% risk reduction for major cardiovascular events [ 11 – 14 ]. The potential benefits of even modest BP reductions emphasize the importance of achieving effective BP control, especially in patients with uncontrolled hypertension. Office BP measurement remains central to the management of elevated BP and continues to guide both the intensification and de-escalation of antihypertensive therapy in the real world. In this study, we aimed to assess individual long-term changes in office BP in a cohort of consecutive patients who underwent RDN for resistant hypertension ten years ago at a single center in Denmark.

[methods] Methods Inclusion and exclusion criteria for undergoing RDN at the time are shown in Table 1 . All patients underwent laboratory testing, 24-h ambulatory BP measurement (24 h-ABPM) to rule out secondary and whitecoat hypertension, radioisotope renography and CT-angiography to assess kidney function and renal artery anatomy, respectively. Table 1 The inclusion and exclusion criteria for RDN therapy in 2012-2013. All patients underwent laboratory testing, radioisotope renography, and a CT-angiography RDN was performed using the first-generation radiofrequency (RF) ablation catheters, the Symplicity Flex Catheter System (Medtronic, Palo Alto, CA, USA), via the femoral route [ 4 ]. A single operator performed all RDN procedures. We aimed to perform at least four circumferential ablations separated longitudinally by 4–5 mm, in each main renal artery proximally to the first bifurcation. All patients were heparinized during the ablation procedure. Energy was delivered for two minutes from a RF generator (part of The Symplicity Renal Denervation System, Medtronic—Ardian 2011) according to an algorithm controlling temperature and impedance. All renal arteries measuring > 4 mm in diameter were targeted for ablation. Procedure-induced visceral pain was treated with synthetic opioids (Fentanyl). The patients were discharged on low dose acetylsalicylic acid for 3 months and continued routine FU in their respective outpatient clinics for at least one year. During the first year of FU, the investigator received information on any events concerning efficacy and safety. Further clinical FU was recorded through our local electronic patient journal, gathering all accessible health information. A full medical history was available for all surviving patients 10 years after RDN treatment. The primary endpoint was the median change in systolic and diastolic office BP from pre-RDN (baseline) to the final year of FU. A secondary endpoint was the corresponding change in office BP from baseline to the mean office BP during the entire FU period. For each year of FU, a mean BP based on all available measures was calculated. All office BP measurements were assessed using the OMRON M6 automatic blood pressure monitor (Omron healthcare Europe B.V. The Netherlands) and registrations were performed by physicians or nurses not involved in this publication. Baseline office BP was defined as the median BP recorded prior to RDN and was compared with the referral office BP. The office BP at the final year of FU was defined as the median of all available measurements during the final year of observation. The mean office BP during the total FU was calculated from all available office BP measurements. If a patient developed a medical condition that could influence BP (e.g. heart failure, hemodialysis, bleeding or bacterial sepsis), the respective BP measurements were censored up to the year before the condition was documented in the patient file. To assess whether the changes in BP could be attributed to changes in the given antihypertensive medication over time, both the total number of antihypertensive medications was recorded as well as the total sum of their corresponding Daily Defined Doses (DDD) for all patients at the time of referral and at the end of the FU period. The latter expresses the change in antihypertensive medication load. The DDD for each medication was calculated using the ATC/DDD Index, as provided by the WHO [ 15 ]. Antihypertensive treatment data were sourced from patient records or the Danish National Prescription Registry, which includes all prescription redemptions in Denmark since 1995, classified by ATC codes. Statistical analysis Due to the small sample size, data were summarized using medians and interquartile ranges (IQR). Changes in BP were assessed using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test. To assess whether baseline BP was associated with the magnitude of BP reduction, both linear regression and Spearman’s rank correlation were used. To address coupling bias, Oldham’s correction was additionally applied in the regression analysis. The mean change from baseline to the averaged post-RDN follow-up BP was estimated using generalized estimating equations (GEE) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) by least squares means. This method accounts for an unequal number of observations per patient and inter-individual correlations of repeated measures. Changes in the total sum of DDD per patient was calculated using the Wilcoxon signed-rank test.

[results] Results Demographics Between January 2012 and October 2013, 19 patients underwent RDN treatment (10 male) with a median age of 63 years (range 35–74 years). The median numbers of antihypertensive drugs at baseline were 5 (range 1–7). Comorbidities included ischemic heart disease ( n = 1), minor stroke without disability ( n = 2), and mild to moderate renal dysfunction ( n = 3). The baseline median systolic and diastolic office BP were 175 mmHg (range 160–212 mmHg) and 96 mmHg (range 84–126 mmHg), respectively. We compared the referral office BP with baseline office BP measured during hospitalization prior to RDN using the Bland Altman test. Both measurements were virtually identical (relative ratio 0.97). Seventeen patients completed a 24 h-ABPM; the median 24 h-ABPM measurement was 169/93 mmHg (range 146–200/81–110 mmHg). In two patients, the 24 h-ABPM was incomplete. Likewise, no significant difference in median office BP was observed between baseline and 3–6 months after RDN. CT-angiography revealed more than two renal arteries (range 3–5) in 11 patients (58%). The median number of ablations in each renal artery was seven (range 0–15). In one case, only unilateral ablation was performed due to anatomical constraints. In five patients, short-lived spasm was observed, but no patient developed persistent arterial stenosis or worsened kidney function during the first year of FU. In two patients, the number of antihypertensive drugs was reduced prior to discharge due to a significant, persistent drop in office BP following the ablation procedure. One patient was discharged without antihypertensive treatment, but treatment was later resumed. Two patients, whose BP had normalized after RDN, were discharged from the outpatient clinic after almost two and three years of FU. Both patients continued follow-up at their general practitioner (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1 Long-term follow-up of a cohort of 19 consecutive patients treated with RDN between 2012–2013 due to resistant hypertension. Abbreviations: CHF: Heart failure; FU: follow-up; GP: General practitioner, HD: Hemodialysis, N: number of patients, RDN: Renal denervation of the sympathetic nerves Long-term follow-up The median FU was nine years (IQR 8–10 years). A flow chart of patient FU is provided in Fig. 1 . Two patients, aged 78 and 79, died nine and ten years after RDN, respectively, both from non-cardiac causes. One patient received hemodialysis ten years after RDN, and two patients developed heart failure, one at six and the other at ten years post-procedure (Fig. 1 ). One patient experienced a transient ischemic attack six years after RDN, another patient a stroke ten years after RDN. None of the patients experienced a major bleeding or a septic episode during FU. Only 13 patients completed a 24-h ABPM 3–6 months after the RDN treatment was given. The median 24 h-ABPM measurement was 160/90 mmHg (range 136–195/74–109 mmHg), which was not different from the values before RDN. During further FU, very few patients underwent 24-h ABPM. Endpoints The individual changes in office BP from baseline to last year of FU for all 19 enrolled patients are depicted in Fig. 2 . There was a statistically significant reduction in both systolic and diastolic blood pressure at the final year of FU. The median systolic BP decreased from 181 mmHg (IQR 28.5) to 142 mmHg (IQR 23.5), with a median reduction of 41 mmHg (IQR 25) ( p < 0.001). Similarly, the median diastolic BP decreased from 97 mmHg (IQR 13.5) to 77 mmHg (IQR 8.5), with a median reduction of 21 mmHg (IQR 9.5) ( p < 0.001). Fig. 2 Change in median office BP between baseline (prior to RDN) and the final year of patient follow- up Whether BP stabilized or continued to decline in the years following RDN was assessed using the method of least squares. The yearly change in office BP from baseline to each FU year throughout the study period is depicted in Fig. 3 . The mean systolic BP reduction was 27.4 mmHg (95% CI 18.9–35.8 mm Hg), corresponding to a relative reduction of 15.0% (95%CI 10.8–19.3%). The diastolic BP was on average reduced to 12.6 mmHg (95%CI 9.1–16.0 mmHg), corresponding to a relative reduction of 12.8% (95%CI 9.7–16.0%). Fig. 3 Changes in office BP from baseline to the average follow-up BP during the total follow-up period. Averages were calculated as the means of annual follow-up BP values for each patient. The yearly change in mean office BP (N=19) from baseline to each FU year throughout the study period, with the range of individual means shown in boxplots and 95% CIs Regression analyses showed no statistically significant association between baseline office BP and the magnitude of BP reduction. While a weak, non-significant positive trend was observed for both systolic BP (Beta ≈ 0.29, p ≈ 0.14, R 2 ≈ 0.12) and diastolic BP (Beta ≈ 0.15, p ≈ 0.35, R 2 ≈ 0.05), this trend disappeared after applying Oldham’s correction. Here, the association between baseline BP and change in BP was essentially null, for both systolic BP (Beta ≈ −0.05, p ≈ 0.81, R 2 ≈ 0.004) and diastolic BP (Beta ≈ −0.08, p ≈ 0.65, R 2 ≈ 0.01). Regarding changes to antihypertensive medication over time, the total number of antihypertensive agents was reduced by one to four in seven patients at the end of FU, increased by two drugs in two patients, and remained unchanged in the remaining ten patients. With respect to changes in the total sum of DDD per patient, a median decrease of −2.12 (range: −9.33 to −0.17 DDD) was found in 12 patients (63%). In contrast, 6 patients (32%) exhibited a median increase of 3.72 (range: 0.33 to 21.08 DDD), including one patient who had developed renal failure during FU with a substantial increase in furosemide dosage (i.e. 750 mg versus 40 mg daily), corresponding to an increase of 18,75 DDD from this drug alone. The overall median reduction in total DDD was −0.68 and not statistically significant ( p = 0.45), as shown in Fig. 4 . After excluding the patient with renal failure, the reduction remained non-significant, with a median decrease of −0.87 DDD ( p = 0.21). Fig. 4 Total defined daily dose (DDD) of antihypertensive medication before and after renal denervation (RDN) in the full cohort ( N = 19). Each line represents an individual patient. The overall change in total DDD was not statistically significant (Wilcoxon p = 0.45)

[discussion] Discussion Only a few studies have been published which try to elucidate the long-term effect and safety of RDN in patients with resistant hypertension [ 6 – 10 ]. Despite many limitations in the present study, a substantial reduction in BP was observed in most patients who had RDN performed 10 years earlier, although the response showed substantial intraindividual variation. Initial regression analyses suggested a weak, non-significant trend indicating that patients with higher baseline BP might experience greater BP reductions following RDN, consistent with prior findings [ 6 , 10 ]. However, after applying Oldham’s correction, this association was no longer evident, and R 2 values were minimal. These results suggest that neither baseline systolic nor diastolic BP reliably predicts the magnitude of BP reduction. Another notable finding was that the BP reduction appeared to be progressive over time and could not be explained by changes in the prescribed antihypertensive treatment – neither in terms of the total number of medications nor in medication load expressed in DDDs. The underlying mechanisms remain speculative but may possibly involve delayed degeneration of renal sympathetic nerve fibers [ 6 , 10 ]. As in other long-term FU studies [ 7 – 10 ], we lacked a control group and cannot determine the true causal effect of RDN. However, the persistent BP reduction observed in most patients — despite adjustments in prescribed medications — may suggest some sustained treatment effect. Several recent RCTs have demonstrated that RDN is efficacious in reducing BP in untreated patients with hypertension. In the SPYRAL HTN-OFF MED trials [ 4 , 16 ] using the newer multielectrode Symplicity Spyral system, a reduction in 24 h-ambulatory systolic BP by 4 to 5 mmHg was demonstrated in drug-native patients compared with the sham control arm. Similar results have been demonstrated in the RADIANCE HTN SOLO and RADIANCE II trials [ 5 , 17 ] using a different ablation approach. In patients treated with prescribed medication for hypertension, however, the efficacy of RDN has been less clear. Several explanations have been proposed, such as changes in lifestyle and medical treatment during follow-up, compliance issues, and importantly individual response to RDN [ 1 , 2 ]. Most of these trials are characterized by short FU periods. During FU, the BP measurement protocol was not changed or improved due to newer or more rigid measurement techniques. BP was measured routinely during outpatient visits or in connection with hospital admissions, although no explicitly standardized measurement protocol was applied. The present results reflect the clinical everyday life of patients followed in an outpatient clinic for resistant hypertension, who had RDN performed 10 years earlier with the aim of improving the control of their markedly elevated BP. Data on the prescribed antihypertensive medications were available throughout the FU in all surviving patients. In accordance with Sesa-Ashton et al. [ 8 ], only minimal medication adjustments were made during FU. The reductions in both systolic and diastolic office BP were greater than those reported in previous long-term FU studies [ 6 – 9 ], which may partly be due to differences in endpoints. Sesa-Ashton et al. [ 8 ] and Al Ghorani et al. [ 9 ] also presented long-term FU data (> 8 years) after RDN. However, both studies included less than half of the original RDN treated population, which may introduce selection bias. A major limitation of the results from the SYMPLICITY HTN-3 study was the lack of operator experience, which meant that relatively few patients technically underwent complete circumferential ablation of the renal arteries [ 18 ]. Newer technology with the use of multi-electrodes as well as a more aggressive approach will probably lead to a more effective destruction of the renal sympathetic nervous system, and thus greater effect on blood pressure, but future and larger studies are needed to elucidate the long-term efficacy of RDN and the clinical trajectory of patients with resistant hypertension. Considering the observations from this long-term study, the future will show whether RDN will become a more widespread treatment option for patients with poorly controlled hypertension, including resistant hypertension. Several limitations of the present study should be acknowledged. First, the study population was small. Second, the data was not derived from a prospective, placebo-controlled design. Third, the observed BP changes were not based on 24 h-ABPM, despite its use in all patients at baselineThe referral of patients with resistant hypertension to our center stopped at the end of 2013, as physicians awaited the results of the SYMPLICITY HTN-3 study. When the negative result of the study became known (April 2014), all Danish centers chose to stop performing the RDN treatment. The planned protocolized follow-up including repeated 24-h ABPM in all patients was abandoned, and subsequently, measurement of 24 h-ABPM was based on clinical indication. Although 24 h-ABPM measurements might be the most reliable and have some prognostic implication, these measurements were thus only available in few patients during further FU in the outpatient clinic. We therefore chose to calculate the average annual office BP, based on values recorded by individual clinicians and documented in medical records, to illustrate overall trends. Office BP remains central in the BP monitoring of most patients with hypertension and is used to both up- and down-regulate antihypertensive medication. The use of an averaged BP for each year of FU in the current study without a control group was an attempt to minimize the influence of a potential placebo effect. Although regression to the mean may contribute to our significant BP reduction findings, the progressive decline observed over multiple years as shown in Fig. 3 argues against this being the sole explanation. Moreover, the decrease in BP was evident in patients who only had minimal changes in antihypertensive medication over time following RDN. In addition, BP tends to increase with age, especially systolic BP, due to age-related changes in the vascular wall [ 19 ], which only reinforces the findings of the current study. Fourth, we acknowledge that BP recordings during the FU occurred under different varying conditions and, for some patients, at irregular time intervals, which on the other hand reflects everyday clinical practice. Approximately 80% of the patients were followed in outpatient clinics for 8–11 years, while the others were transferred to their general practitioner due to normalization of BP. Two patients were followed for only 2 and 3 years after RDN, respectively. In both cases, their BP had normalized, and they requested continued FU and BP monitoring at their general practitioner. Neither of the two patients had ever been hospitalized. Fifth, validation of medication adherence in primary care was not performed. Finally, heart failure is a well-known confounder. We therefore reviewed the medical records and excluded BP measurements from the time when a patient developed clinical heart failure or experienced a decline in left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) as assessed by echocardiography. Two patients, aged 67 and 78 years, developed heart failure 11 and 7 years after the RDN, respectively, and their BP measurements were subsequently censored. In both patients, BP was low at the time of heart failure.

[conclusion] Conclusions In this cohort of patients with resistant hypertension, office BP was significantly reduced ten years after catheter-based renal denervation. The reduction appeared to persist and even progress over time, despite minor changes in prescribed medical treatment. The present study is unable to demonstrate a specific causal relationship between RDN and the progressive reduction in office BP during FU. Considering the present observations, further long-term sham-controlled follow-up studies will be needed, especially given the high prevalence of uncontrolled hypertension in the general population and the fact that many patients fail to achieve recommended BP targets in parallel with lifestyle and medical interventions.

retrieved_papers.title: Renal denervation in dialysis patients: long-term outcomes in a real-world setting.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Resistant hypertension is common in patients with end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) who are on dialysis. Experience with percutaneous renal denervation (RDN) in these patients is limited and generally has short follow-up periods, primarily focusing on blood pressure (BP) control. In particular, the effects of RDN on anaemia, chronic kidney disease-mineral and bone disorder (CKD-MBD) and dialysis tolerance remain unexamined.
We report our experience with RDN in 14 dialysis patients [7 on haemodialysis (HD) and 7 on peritoneal dialysis (PD)] with a follow-up after RDN of 3-84 months. Six patients received kidney transplantation during follow-up.
BP and the number of antihypertensive drugs decreased (P = .008 and P = .018, respectively). RDN was equally effective in both HD and PD. In nearly all transplanted patients, BP normalized and the number of antihypertensive drugs was reduced after kidney transplantation. RDN did not alter treatment schedules for HD or PD and no episodes of acute intradialytic hypotension were reported. Additionally, there were no significant changes in treatments for anaemia or CKD-MBD. Only one patient had an RDN procedural complication.
Our experience with RDN in dialysis patients suggests beneficial effects in both HD and PD. BP improved significantly, with no observed issues related to the typical clinical problems faced by these patients, such as anaemia, CKD-MBD and dialysis tolerance. Furthermore, in those patients who received a kidney transplant, we noted a further improvement in BP control.

[Introduction] INTRODUCTION Hypertension that is resistant to treatment is frequently observed in patients with end-stage kidney disease (ESKD) undergoing dialysis. Studies have shown that the prevalence of resistant hypertension in this population ranges from 18% to 42% [ 1 ]. These patients experience elevated mortality rates primarily due to cardiovascular (CV) diseases [ 2 ], and for this reason, they may be excluded from transplantation lists until their blood pressure (BP) is controlled. In chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients, sympathetic activity progressively increases with the severity of renal dysfunction, reaching its highest levels in ESKD [ 3 ]. This overactivity is a major driver of hypertension in both CKD and ESKD. Additionally, the autonomic system, beyond its role in regulating BP and cardiac rhythm, also modulates the secretion of erythropoietin and parathyroid hormone [ 4–6 ]. Renal sympathectomy, obtained with percutaneous renal denervation (RDN), has been developed to reduce BP in resistant and uncontrolled hypertension. After the first randomized trial on percutaneous catheter–based RDN in 2010 [ 7 ], several others have been performed, and in November 2023, the US Food and Drug Administration approved this technique as an adjunctive treatment option when lifestyle changes and medication do not result in adequate BP control [ 8 ]. Until now, CKD with a glomerular filtration rate (GFR) <40 ml/min/1.73 m 2 has been an exclusion criterion of RDN trials. A European consensus statement concluded that it is not advised to perform RDN in kidney transplant recipients or patients with severely impaired kidney function (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes stage G4 and G5) or who require haemodialysis (HD) [ 9 ]. The recent European Society of Cardiology guidelines state that RDN is not recommended in patients with an eGFR <40 ml/min/1.73 m 2 [ 10 ]. Thus the experience of CKD patients treated with RDN is limited to a few small studies of hypertensive CKD patients [ 11–13 ]. Even more limited is the experience in ESKD treated with HD, accounting for only 39 published patients—four case reports [ 14–17 ] and five small series or cohorts [ 18–22 ] ( Supplementary Table S1 ). These reports had a short follow-up of 6–24 months and the main emphasis was on BP control. Clinical issues related to HD tolerance and treatment per se and CV events were only scantly [ 21 ] or not at all analysed [ 18–20 , 22 ]. Furthermore, the short follow-up prevented the possibility of investigating the impact of RDN on the whole clinical ‘journey’ of a renal patient, which includes changes in dialysis techniques and transplantation. Moreover, the experience of RDN in peritoneal dialysis (PD) patients is even smaller than within HD, accounting for only five patients [ 19 ]. Since 2015, 14 of our ESKD patients on dialysis have undergone RDN. In this article we analyse the clinical course in this case series, emphasizing not only the effect on BP, but also on the main clinical issues faced by dialysis patients, namely anaemia, chronic kidney disease–mineral and bone disorder (CKD-MBD), dialysis tolerance and residual diuresis.

Studies have shown that the prevalence of resistant hypertension in this population ranges from 18% to 42% [ 1 ]. These patients experience elevated mortality rates primarily due to cardiovascular (CV) diseases [ 2 ], and for this reason, they may be excluded from transplantation lists until their blood pressure (BP) is controlled. In chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients, sympathetic activity progressively increases with the severity of renal dysfunction, reaching its highest levels in ESKD [ 3 ]. This overactivity is a major driver of hypertension in both CKD and ESKD. Additionally, the autonomic system, beyond its role in regulating BP and cardiac rhythm, also modulates the secretion of erythropoietin and parathyroid hormone [ 4–6 ]. Renal sympathectomy, obtained with percutaneous renal denervation (RDN), has been developed to reduce BP in resistant and uncontrolled hypertension. After the first randomized trial on percutaneous catheter–based RDN in 2010 [ 7 ], several others have been performed, and in November 2023, the US Food and Drug Administration approved this technique as an adjunctive treatment option when lifestyle changes and medication do not result in adequate BP control [ 8 ]. Until now, CKD with a glomerular filtration rate (GFR) <40 ml/min/1.73 m 2 has been an exclusion criterion of RDN trials. A European consensus statement concluded that it is not advised to perform RDN in kidney transplant recipients or patients with severely impaired kidney function (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes stage G4 and G5) or who require haemodialysis (HD) [ 9 ]. The recent European Society of Cardiology guidelines state that RDN is not recommended in patients with an eGFR <40 ml/min/1.73 m 2 [ 10 ]. Thus the experience of CKD patients treated with RDN is limited to a few small studies of hypertensive CKD patients [ 11–13 ]. Even more limited is the experience in ESKD treated with HD, accounting for only 39 published patients—four case reports [ 14–17 ] and five small series or cohorts [ 18–22 ] ( Supplementary Table S1 ). These reports had a short follow-up of 6–24 months and the main emphasis was on BP control. Clinical issues related to HD tolerance and treatment per se and CV events were only scantly [ 21 ] or not at all analysed [ 18–20 , 22 ]. Furthermore, the short follow-up prevented the possibility of investigating the impact of RDN on the whole clinical ‘journey’ of a renal patient, which includes changes in dialysis techniques and transplantation. Moreover, the experience of RDN in peritoneal dialysis (PD) patients is even smaller than within HD, accounting for only five patients [ 19 ]. Since 2015, 14 of our ESKD patients on dialysis have undergone RDN. In this article we analyse the clinical course in this case series, emphasizing not only the effect on BP, but also on the main clinical issues faced by dialysis patients, namely anaemia, chronic kidney disease–mineral and bone disorder (CKD-MBD), dialysis tolerance and residual diuresis.

[methods] MATERIALS AND METHODS RDN has been performed in 14 of our ESKD patients on dialysis therapy and clinically stable for at least 6 months (7 HD patients and 7 PD patients). All HD patients received three 4-h dialysis sessions per week. In October 2024, the follow-up ranged from 3 to 84 months and eight of them had a follow-up >2 years; six patients received a kidney transplant. Patients on HD or PD with resistant hypertension were proposed for RDN only after verifying with bioimpedance that they were not overhydrated and that the depuration indexes were in accordance with guidelines. Each patient was evaluated jointly for RDN by the dedicated multidisciplinary team of the hospital (the GITIAR team) composed of nephrologists, cardiologists and hypertension specialists. The selection of patients with resistant hypertension for RDN was done following a flow chart like the one suggested in the position paper of the Italian Society of Arterial Hypertension [ 23 ] ( Supplementary Fig. S1 ). The protocol includes clinical evaluation and imaging of the renal arteries. RDN is not executed in patients who have, at renal angiogram performed during the RDN procedure, a haemodynamically significant renal artery stenosis (stenosis diameter >70%) with the demonstration of a significant (>20 mmHg) trans-lesional pressure gradient [ 24 ] or a renal artery diameter <2 mm. The protocol was approved by the local ethics committee (CESC-2361) and by the Department of Health of the Veneto Region [ 13 ]. All patients signed an informed consent and were enrolled in the Global Symplicity Registry (GSR) [ 25 ]. The bilateral RDN procedure has been previously described [ 13 ]. A unipolar catheter (Symplicity Flex; Medtronic, Minneapolis, MN, USA) was used in one patient (patient 1), while the others were treated with a tetrapolar catheter (Symplicity Spyral; Medtronic). According to the protocol, follow-up visits by the cardiologist are scheduled at 3, 6 and 12 months post-procedure. Office BP (OBP) was measured at each visit ( Supplementary Table S2 ). Assessment of medication intake was determined through direct questioning. The protocol also includes the assessment of 24-h ambulatory BP monitoring; however, not all patients agreed to undergo it at some of the scheduled times. Furthermore, during the follow-up after the first year, only OBP measurements were taken. Therefore, for the sake of consistency, only OBP values are presented. OBP is the mean of three recordings taken while sitting after a 5-minute rest using a validated sphygmomanometer. In HD patients, OBP was measured before the mid-week dialysis session. Other clinical data in the first 12 months following RDN were collected as part of routine clinical practice at each dialysis session or outpatient visit during follow-up, conducted by the nephrologists responsible for the patients. The medical information regarding the patients’ clinical status, OBP, medication regimen, adherence to antihypertensive treatment, episodes of intradialytic hypotension, changes in the dialysis schedule or in dialysis technique, residual diuresis (qualitatively reported but not quantified through 24-h urine collection), anaemia management and treatment for CKD-MBD, cardiac arrhythmias and CV events was obtained from clinical records and through a questionnaire distributed to the nephrologists overseeing the patients ( Supplementary Material ). In the questionnaires, nephrologists were asked to provide a clinical assessment of these aspects. Statistical analysis of the trajectory of mean arterial pressure and the number of medications during follow-up was performed using a linear mixed effects model with a random intercept for each patient to account for repeated measurements. Fixed effects included time, clinical status (HD, PD, transplant), age at baseline, sex and the presence of diabetes. Additionally, a further analysis was conducted excluding patients with only two observations, to verify the robustness of the results. The open-source lme4 package in R version 4.1.1 (R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria) was used.

[results] RESULTS Patient characteristics The patients’ ages ranged from 21 to 81 years. Apart from two patients (ages 78 and 81 years), the others were <56 years of age. Sex, presence of diabetes, causes of ESKD, changes in dialysis technique and overall follow-up are shown in Table 1 . In one patient (patient 0), who had good BP control with two drugs, RDN was not performed for resistant hypertension but for a very high CV risk. Table 1: Demographic and general clinical data of dialysis patients who underwent RDN. Patient Sex Age (years) a ESKD cause Diabetes Dialysis type at RDN Changed RRT during follow-up Overall follow-up (months) 1 M 54 Diabetic nephropathy Yes (type 2) HD 7 b 2 M 41 IgAN No HD Tx 84 3 M 21 IgAN No HD Tx 48 4 M 44 Single kidney. Unknown No PD HD, Tx 68 5 M 81 Hypertensive nephrosclerosis No PD 48 b 6 M 32 Hypertensive nephrosclerosis No HD Tx 42 7 M 27 Unknown No HD Tx 42 8 F 50 Hypertensive nephrosclerosis No PD 24 9 F 46 Hypertensive nephrosclerosis No PD Tx 24 10 M 78 Hypertensive nephrosclerosis No PD 18 11 M 56 IgAN Yes HD 15 12 M 53 Diabetic nephropathy Yes (type 1) HD 12 13 M 49 Hypertensive nephrosclerosis and NSAID abuse No PD 3 14 M 42 Fibrillary glomerulopathy No PD 3 a At RDN. b Deceased. IgAN: immunoglobulin A nephropathy; NSAID: non-steroidal inflammatory drugs; RRT: renal replacement therapy; Tx: kidney transplantation. Effect on BP and treatment Fig. 1 shows the time course of mean OBP [(systolic BP + 2 diastolic BP)/3] in each patient during the entire follow-up period. Supplementary Figs. S2 – S4 show the number of antihypertensive agents at different time points for each patient. Figure 1: Time course of mean OBP after RDN in dialysis patients. Mean OBP is [(systolic BP + 2 diastolic BP)/3]. The overall follow-ups of the 14 patients are shown; the red lines refer to HD, the yellow lines refer to PD and the blue lines refer to kidney transplantation. The horizontal black line corresponds to an OBP of 140/80 mmHg. Considering the entire follow-up period, the analysis showed that after RDN there was a significant reduction over time in mean OBP (−0.3 mmHg/month; P = .008) and in the number of antihypertensive medications (−0.025 medications/month; P = .018), after adjusting for all covariates [clinical status (HD, PD or transplant), age at baseline, sex and diabetes] (Figs. 2 and 3 ). However, it is clear that the OBP reduction occurred in the initial 24 months of follow-up (≈30 mmHg; adjusted for the above covariates −1.27 mmHg/month; P = 2.43e-06), while it remained substantially stable in the rest of the follow-up period. A reduction in the pill burden was observed across almost all patients. Figure 2: Trajectory of mean OBP after RDN during the entire follow-up period. Mean OBP decreased by 0.3 mmHg/month ( P = .008). The shadow area shows confidence intervals. Figure 3: Trajectory of the number of drugs after RDN during the entire follow-up period. The number of drugs decreased by 0.025 pills/month ( P = .018). The shadow area shows confidence intervals. Focusing solely on the dialysis follow-up, patients on HD showed a trend towards a greater reduction in mean OBP compared with PD patients (−1.46 mmHg/month versus 0.52 mmHg/month, respectively; P = .08443, not significant). Notably, in transplanted patients there was a further reduction in OBP (0.22 mmHg/month; P = .00374), with nearly all patients almost normalizing their BP ( Supplementary Fig. S3 ). Effect on the clinical issues of dialysis patients The treatment schedules for HD and PD were not affected by RDN, and episodes of acute intradialytic hypotension were not observed during the follow-up of HD patients (Table 2 ). Only one patient had to be switched from PD to HD, and this was due to severe peritonitis, which was independent of BP control and RDN. Residual diuresis in dialysis patients was not significantly altered by RDN (Table 2 ). The nephrologists responsible for the clinical follow-up of patients reported no issues or variations in anaemia and CKD-MBD parameters and treatment, aside from a few cases (Tables 2 and 3 ). Therefore, there was no indication of any worsening or improvement trend in these clinical issues after RDN. Table 2: Clinical events during follow-up of dialysis patients who underwent RDN. a Follow-up on dialysis after RDN. b On dialysis. c Deceased. Events and changes that occurred during the follow-up are highlighted in yellow. AMI: acute myocardial infarction; HF: heart failure. All anuric patients were anuric at the time of RDN. Table 3: Clinical events during the follow-up after transplantation in d ialysis patients who underwent RDN and subsequently received a transplant. Patient Follow-up (months) a BP control b New heart arrhythmias CV events CKD-MBD control Anaemia control 2 82 Improved No No Not varied Not varied 3 41 Improved No No Not varied Not varied 4 26 Improved No No Not varied Not varied 6 34 Improved No No Not varied Not varied 7 6 Improved No No Not varied Not varied 9 12 Improved No No Not varied Not varied a After transplantation. b Compared with BP control on dialysis. Safety of RDN One patient, an 81-year-old man, and the oldest person in the study, developed chronic atrial fibrillation. He subsequently died from acute myocardial infarction 48 months after RDN. Another patient died after only 7 months post-RDN, but from an unrelated cause. Specifically, he developed pneumonitis and sepsis following a femoral neck fracture and during hospitalization he experienced a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Regarding the periprocedural safety of the RDN treatment, no complications were observed except in one patient who developed a femoral pseudo-aneurysm.

[discussion] DISCUSSION One of the primary contributors to hypertension in patients undergoing dialysis is sodium retention and an increase in blood volume [ 1 , 26 ]. Other contributors to hypertension include endothelial dysfunction, activation of the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system and heightened activity of the sympathetic nervous system. The influence of sympathetic nerve factors is underscored by findings that shed light on how noradrenergic activation may occur [ 27 ]. For instance, the native kidneys can transmit afferent nerve signals to the central nervous system, which can trigger sympathetic overactivity. Moreover, sympathetic nervous system activity tends to increase as CKD progresses and afferent renal nerves may exert an excitatory effect on central sympathetic output in response to renal injury [ 3 ]. Ultimately, both renal sympathetic efferent and afferent nerves significantly impact the onset, persistence and worsening of high BP commonly seen in ESKD patients, frequently resulting in resistant hypertension [ 26 ]. This is the rationale that has led to the use of RDN for the treatment of resistant hypertension, a treatment that might be highly effective in patients with ESKD. However, few studies conducted on small series or cohorts of dialysis patients have addressed the effect of percutaneous RDN on resistant hypertension. They have shown that BP and/or the number of antihypertensive medications generally improved. However, all these studies had a relatively short duration, lasting no longer than 6–24 months ( Supplementary Table S1 ). Patients with ESKD often have a complex and longer clinical history, sometimes transitioning between the two forms of dialysis (HD or PD) or to and from transplantation. While the mid-term effects of RDN on BP control have been investigated, any effects on anaemia and CKD-MBD treatments and dialysis tolerance have not been examined. We treated 14 dialysis patients with RDN from 2015 to October 2024, of which 7 were on PD. On average, we treat 220 HD patients and 45 PD patients in our wards. Thus the ecological prevalence of RDN is 3.6% for HD and 15.5% for PD. This finding may seem surprising; however, the burden of hypertension in PD patients is like that observed in HD patients. According to the Italian Cooperative Peritoneal Dialysis Study Group, in a cohort of 504 PD patients, the prevalence of hypertension was 88%, with 79% of drug-treated hypertensive patients having uncontrolled clinic BP [ 28 ]. Furthermore, after the initial 6–12 months of improved BP following the initiation of PD, a progressive deterioration in BP control occurs [ 29 ]. As mentioned, sodium retention and increased fluid volume are the main contributors to hypertension in patients undergoing dialysis [ 25 ]. This is particularly relevant for PD patients, as volume overload is common due to liberal fluid intake and the loss of residual renal function. Studies have demonstrated that alleviating volume overload by eliminating excess sodium and adjusting target dry weight can lead to improvements in BP for those receiving dialysis [ 30 , 31 ]. However, during the selection phase of dialysis patients for eligibility for RDN, we specifically focused on excluding overhydration as a factor contributing to resistant hypertension, particularly in PD patients. The sustained effect of RDN on BP in PD patients over time supports the view that this effect is not dependent on any modification of volume load. Similar to other studies, we observed a favourable effect of RDN on BP and on the number of medications (Figs. 2 and 3 ). Patient 12 did not show any improvement, likely due to poor adherence (Fig. 1 ). Experimental and clinical studies have demonstrated the role of adrenergic activation in the risk of ventricular hypertrophy and arrhythmogenesis [ 32–34 ]. Clinical studies have demonstrated that RDN reduces the incidence of atrial fibrillation and CV events, even in the absence of a significant antihypertensive effect [ 35 ]. With this background and the aim of reducing the elevated CV risk, we treated patient 10 with RDN, the only patient who did not present with resistant hypertension. Nevertheless, autonomic regulation of the heart and circulation undergoes significant and complex changes in individuals with CKD and ESKD, affecting both parasympathetic and sympathetic control over the heart and peripheral blood vessels. We were concerned that, because of such derangements, acute intradialytic hypotensive episodes, major cardiac arrhythmias and fatal and non-fatal CV events might occur in dialysis patients. However, during follow-up after RDN, patients did not experience any of these conditions. In HD patients, episodes of acute intradialytic hypotension were not observed during the follow-up period (Table 2 ). We recorded only two deaths after 7 and 48 months (patient 1 due to sepsis and patient 5 due to acute myocardial infarction), a number lower than the expected 40% 5-year mortality rate observed in dialysis patients [ 36 ]. Although BP reduction after RDN was associated with a lower incidence of long-term CV events in patients with resistant hypertension compared with non-responders [ 37 ], we believe that the low number of deaths in our cohort is primarily due to selection bias, as only patients with better survival prospects and/or younger age were selected for the RDN procedure. Renal nerves influence fluid volume regulation through renal sympathetic efferent nerves by increasing the activity of the systemic renin–angiotensin system, renin secretion and sodium absorption [ 38 ]. Thus RDN may impair fluid control in some way. We explored this possibility by evaluating whether diuresis, which serves as a proxy for fluid control and residual renal function, was altered after RDN. However, no variations were observed. The sympathetic nervous system is involved in bone metabolism, as evidenced by the high bone mass phenotype observed in mice with low sympathetic activity [ 39 ]. Notably, in dialysis patients, a connection has been identified between sympathetic hyperactivity due to uraemia and CKD-MBD [ 5 , 6 ]. Therefore, we investigated whether RDN had any effect on secondary hyperparathyroidism and the treatment of CKD-MBD. Our clinical experience did not reveal any negative trends associated with RDN (Tables 2 and 3 ). Since erythropoiesis is impaired when the kidney is denervated due to reduced erythropoietin production in response to hypoxia [ 4 ], we also examined whether there were any clinical issues with treatment for anaemia. Again, RDN showed no impact on this aspect (Table 2 ). During follow-up, six dialysis patients received kidney transplants. The significant BP improvement after RDN allowed them to re-enter the kidney transplant list, from which they had previously been excluded for the high CV and peri-procedural bleeding risks related to poorly controlled hypertension. In these patients, CKD-MBD, anaemia and its treatment did not require any special attention (Table 3 ). Interestingly, they experienced further improvements in OBP and required fewer antihypertensive medications over time ( Supplementary Figs.
